channel.title: tn1ck.com
channel.description: Blog posts by Tom Nick about programming, design, and other topics.

item.title: Why some images look brighter than your screen
item.description: How HDR images can make logos and highlights appear unnaturally bright, and how to create them yourself.

item.title: Claude Code made me love meetings again
item.description: AI coding tools reduced my dependence on deep flow and gave me the capacity to enjoy spontaneous meetings again.

item.title: If it isn't visible, it's probably broken
item.description: A framework for thinking about visibility: who can see issues, how hard it is to verify, and how often anything gets checked.

item.title: How I stopped worrying and learned to love the easy fix
item.description: On the balance between perfect solutions and pragmatic fixes in software engineering

item.title: Footnotes for your React / Next.js blog
item.description: Footnotes for your React / Next.js blog

item.title: Generating sudokus for fun and no profit
item.description: A guide on how to generate sudokus of any difficulty.

item.title: Safeguarding changes using the plan-execute pattern
item.description: The plan-execute pattern is a way to preview changes before they are applied. This blog post shows how to use this pattern for database updates.

item.title: Recreating the New Dropbox Header Animation
item.description: Dropbox just revamped their branding - and their website. The new header uses a cool clipping effect, which we’ll recreate.

item.title: Finding the optimal solution for the numbers game
item.description: One of the games bored students play is the 'Numbers Game,' also known as 'Take Tens' or in German, 'Zahlenspiel.' I once implemented it and wrote a solver to find the minimum number of steps required to solve the game. Here is the solution.

item.title: The MIU System
item.description: The first exercise introduced in Gödel, Escher, Bach is the MIU System. This post describes the system and how to solve it.

item.title: Place 101 at Hashcode 2017
item.description: Hashcode is an international programming competition where teams, with a maximum of 4 people, have to solve a complex problem in under 4 hours. We reached place 101 in the 2017 competition; this is a short summary of our experience.
